metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: The plan specifies that `Unavailable` carries an ISO `accruing_since` field (and AC2 asserts it is non-empty), but never defines where this timestamp originates — whether it is a static field on `MetricSpec` (the metric's introduction date, set at definition time), dynamically computed (e.g., 'now' when compute returns no data), or read from some external record. This leaves the field semantically undefined and the AC unverifiable.
findings.location: Approach section; AC2 — 'A metric with no data returns Unavailable carrying a non-empty reason and an ISO accruing_since'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `accruing_since: str` to `MetricSpec` as a required ISO-date field (e.g. '2025-01-15') set at registry definition time, representing when the metric began accruing. `Unavailable` then reads it from the spec. This makes the field durable, meaningful, and trivially testable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach or registry.py description that the registry runner catches exceptions from `compute()` and wraps them as `Unavailable(reason=str(exc), accruing_since=...)`, so a failing data source degrades gracefully per-metric rather than crashing the renderer.
evidence: 'a metric with no data returns Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)' — plan specifies the no-data case but not the exception case
AC2 only asserts 'compute returns no data → Unavailable'; no AC covers compute raising an exception
No exception-catching contract is described in MetricSpec, REGISTRY, or the compute field specification
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a metric with no data returns Unavailable, but does not specify what happens when compute() raises an exception (e.g., subprocess I/O error, sidecar unreachable). If exceptions propagate unhandled, the renderer crashes rather than gracefully degrading per-metric — breaking the core 'command written once, never crashes on missing signal' promise.
findings.location: Approach section / Files: registry.py / AC line 2 ('A metric with no data returns Unavailable')
scenarios: A git-based metric's subprocess call fails (git not found, repo corrupt) — compute() raises CalledProcessError, renderer crashes
A sidecar metric's HTTP call times out — compute() raises TimeoutError, whole command fails instead of surfacing per-metric Unavailable
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'The registry runner wraps each compute() call in a try/except and converts any raised exception to Unavailable(reason=repr(exc), accruing_since=<now or epoch>), so a broken data source never propagates past the per-metric boundary.' Add a corresponding AC or sub-bullet under AC2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (Approach or Tests) the exception-fallback contract for `compute()`: either exceptions propagate to the caller, or the registry wraps them into `Unavailable(reason=str(exc), accruing_since=...)` — and add an AC or test covering that path.
evidence: Approach: 'a metric with no data yet returns `Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)` rather than being absent'
Tests: 'a metric whose compute returns no data yields `Unavailable`' — the test covers only the no-data path
No plan text addresses what happens when compute() raises (e.g., IOError, timeout, sidecar unreachable); the fallback is wholly unspecified
findings.finding: The plan defines `Unavailable` only for the 'no data' case and specifies no fallback or error contract when `compute()` raises an exception.
scenarios: A sidecar source is temporarily unreachable; compute() raises ConnectionError; the registry layer has no specified behavior, leaving the CLI to crash or handle it ad-hoc
An implementor wraps all exceptions into Unavailable; another wraps only empty results — inconsistent behavior with no plan guidance

findings.finding: No fallback is specified for a `compute` callable that raises an exception at runtime (as distinct from returning no data). The plan only defines the no-data → `Unavailable` path; a runtime error in `compute` has no specified handling.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a metric with no data yet returns Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)'

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The `compute` field is named in `MetricSpec` but its callable signature — what arguments it receives, what it must return when data is present, and whether it returns `Unavailable`/`None`/raises when data is absent — is never specified. The plan says 'a metric with no data returns `Unavailable`' without defining whether `compute` itself produces `Unavailable`, returns `None` (and the registry wraps it), or raises.
findings.location: Files section / registry.py — `MetricSpec (…compute)`; Approach — 'a metric with no data yet returns Unavailable(reason, accruing_since)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'compute: Callable[[], MetricValue | None] — returns a MetricValue when data is present, or None when unavailable; the registry wraps a None return into Unavailable(reason=<MetricSpec.unavailable_reason>, accruing_since=<MetricSpec.accruing_since>). Exceptions propagate uncaught (or are caught and re-raised as a distinct ErrorValue — TBD).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact `accruing_since` format in the plan (e.g. 'UTC ISO 8601 datetime string, e.g. 2024-01-15T00:00:00Z') so the AC assertion and implementation are unambiguous.
evidence: 'an ISO accruing_since' — 'ISO' alone covers ISO 8601 date, datetime, with/without timezone offset, which is ambiguous for both implementation and test assertion
findings.finding: The `accruing_since` field is described only as 'ISO' without specifying the exact format (e.g. ISO 8601 datetime string with UTC timezone vs. date-only vs. local time), leaving an executor to guess the format string and making the AC's 'ISO accruing_since' assertion imprecise.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'a non-empty reason and an ISO accruing_since'
scenarios: Executor uses datetime.date.isoformat() (date-only) while the reviewer or another consumer expects a full datetime with UTC offset — test passes but interop breaks.
AC assertion is written as a string-format check that accepts date-only strings, masking a timezone-naive datetime bug.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC2, replace 'an ISO accruing_since' with 'an ISO 8601 UTC datetime string for accruing_since (e.g. datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z" or datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat())' and add a format-regex assertion in the test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan that `is_authoritative()` must be implemented as a membership test against a named constant (e.g., `_AUTHORITATIVE_SOURCES: frozenset[Source]`) defined in `registry.py`, not as an ad-hoc inline set, so adding a source requires a deliberate update to that constant.
evidence: AC-3: '`is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` is False and an authoritative aggregation excludes classified values — unit test asserts a mixed input yields a series containing only `source in {structural,git,sidecar,snapshot}`'
The plan does not specify that the set `{structural,git,sidecar,snapshot}` inside `is_authoritative()` must be derived from the same constant as the `source` enum — they could diverge silently
No plan text mandates that adding a new source to the enum automatically updates the authoritative set or requires a deliberate exclusion
findings.finding: The `is_authoritative()` segregation rule (the set of authoritative source values) is stated only in the Tests/AC prose and is never specified to be derived from or co-located with the `source` enum definition in `registry.py`.
scenarios: A new source 'operational' is added to the source enum but `is_authoritative` is not updated; operational data incorrectly passes the authoritative filter
The test hardcodes the set independently of the implementation, masking a future divergence

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the `source` enum as closed/exhaustive (with `is_authoritative` raising on unknown values) or explicitly open (with a stated default behavior for unknown sources) in the plan.
evidence: 'source in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}' — no statement that this list is exhaustive or that additions require a plan amendment
'is_authoritative' helper implicitly partitions on this set, so an undeclared source would have undefined authoritative status
findings.finding: The `source` enum values ({structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}) are listed but not explicitly declared as a closed/exhaustive set, leaving an executor uncertain whether adding a new source type requires updating the plan or whether the set is intentionally open for extension.
findings.location: Approach section: 'source in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}'
scenarios: A future executor adds a new source type (e.g. 'manual') without knowing whether it should be authoritative, silently including it in rollups that should exclude it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Approach: 'The source enum is closed; is_authoritative raises ValueError for any source not in this set, so new sources require an explicit plan amendment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `Source` and `Confidence` as `Literal` types or `enum.Enum` classes in `registry.py` and reference them in `MetricSpec` field annotations, so the full vocabulary is machine-checkable and co-located with the schema.
evidence: Plan prose: '`source` in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified} and `confidence` in {high, classified} ride on every value'
No Literal, Enum, or frozenset definition is specified for either set anywhere in the plan
Search for 'source.*structural.*git.*sidecar' in src/rebar returns no matches — no existing definition to inherit from
registry.py is named as the file but the plan only describes MetricSpec fields, not the enum types themselves
findings.finding: The `source` and `confidence` enum vocabularies are named in prose but never formally defined as a type/constant in any specified file — an implementor has no single authoritative definition to reference or extend.
scenarios: An implementor writes `source: str` with no constraint; a downstream caller passes 'Structural' (capitalized) and is_authoritative silently returns False
A future signal is added with a misspelled source string; no validation catches it at registration time
findings.suggested_fix: In registry.py add: `Source = Literal['structural','git','sidecar','snapshot','backfill_classified']` and `Confidence = Literal['high','classified']`, then type MetricSpec's fields as `source: Source` and `confidence: Confidence`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC1) that verifies every REGISTRY entry's `compute` field is present and callable (e.g., `callable(spec.compute)` for all specs).
evidence: 'MetricSpec (id, lens, source, confidence, compute)' — Approach/What name compute as a required field
AC1 asserts 'every REGISTRY entry has a unique id, a lens, a source, and a confidence' — compute is absent from this list
No other AC references compute
findings.finding: The 'compute' field of MetricSpec is described in What, Files, and Approach but no acceptance criterion verifies that it exists, has the correct signature, or is exercised — it is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Files: registry.py / Approach: 'MetricSpec (id, lens, source, confidence, compute)' / AC1 (only checks id, lens, source, confidence)
scenarios: An implementer could ship MetricSpec without a compute field or with a no-op lambda and all ACs would still pass.
Future metrics added to the REGISTRY could have malformed or missing compute callables with no test gate catching it.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: '…and a `compute` callable — asserted by `all(callable(spec.compute) for spec in REGISTRY)`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that a `MetricValue` result carries non-empty `source` and `confidence` fields consistent with the declared enumerations.
evidence: Files section: 'MetricValue/Unavailable result types' — lists MetricValue as a named deliverable
Approach: 'source and confidence labels ride on every value' implies MetricValue carries these fields
AC2 only verifies Unavailable fields (reason, accruing_since); AC3 only verifies source filtering — neither directly asserts MetricValue's shape
findings.finding: The 'MetricValue' result type is described as a deliverable in Files/Approach but has no acceptance criterion covering it, making it an orphan deliverable.
findings.location: Files: registry.py ('MetricValue/Unavailable result types') / Tests section / ACs
scenarios: MetricValue could be shipped as a plain dict or namedtuple missing the source/confidence fields that the Approach promises, with no AC catching the gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC4: 'A metric whose compute returns data yields a MetricValue with source in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified} and confidence in {high, classified} — unit test asserts both fields present and within the declared sets.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section documenting the source/confidence label contract, the Unavailable state semantics, and the is_authoritative segregation rule so future contributors extend the registry without breaking downstream aggregation invariants.
evidence: 'source in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified} and confidence in {high, classified} ride on every value so downstream aggregation can keep high-confidence structural series apart'
'rendering iterates the registry, and a metric with no data yet returns Unavailable'
No ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc file listed in the ## Files section
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-cutting architectural contract — source/confidence labels, the Unavailable state shape, and the is_authoritative segregation rule — that downstream consumers (CLI rendering, aggregation) must conform to, but no ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update is specified to capture this decision.
findings.location: ## Approach / ## Files (registry.py, __init__.py) — no doc update mentioned
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new source type (e.g., llm_enriched) without knowing the authoritative-vs-classified contract, silently breaking aggregation invariants.
A new CLI consumer doesn't know the Unavailable shape contract and defensively ignores it, re-introducing the absent-metric problem the plan solves.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (e.g., docs/adr/0NNN-metric-registry-contract.md) or an AGENTS.md section covering: (1) the closed set of source values and what each means, (2) confidence levels and their aggregation semantics, (3) the Unavailable shape and when compute must return it, (4) the is_authoritative rule. List this file in ## Files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that at least two distinct, current (not hypothetical) consumers of `REGISTRY` / `MetricSpec` / `MetricValue` are named in the plan or an accompanying AC; if only one command currently consumes the registry, consider deferring the full package until a second call-site materialises (Rule-of-Three).
evidence: Plan states 'the command is written once and never modified as new signals accrue' — exactly one consumer ('the command') is named.
grep for MetricSpec, MetricValue, accruing_since, is_authoritative, backfill_classified in src/rebar all return zero matches — no existing call-sites.
The plan's justification ('a new signal lights up by populating its source with no CLI change') is an explicitly future/hypothetical benefit, not a current done-definition requirement.
Rule-of-Three: >=3 existing call-sites required before an abstraction is non-premature; count here is 1.
findings.finding: The five-symbol abstraction package (`MetricSpec`, `REGISTRY`, `MetricValue`, `Unavailable`, `is_authoritative`) is introduced for a single named consumer ('the command'), with no existing or planned second call-site grounded in the current done-definition — violating Rule-of-Three for new abstractions.
scenarios: If the command is the only consumer at ship time, the registry package adds indirection and test overhead for no current benefit — the same behavior could be inline in the command with a plain dict-of-callables.
If 'the command' is cancelled or merged into another surface, the abstraction is dead code from day one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether `is_authoritative()` needs to be a named exported function now, or whether its logic can live inline in the single rollup helper until a second caller exists.
evidence: AC 3: '`is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` is False and an authoritative aggregation excludes classified values — unit test asserts a mixed input yields a series containing only `source in {structural,git,sidecar,snapshot}`.'
grep for is_authoritative in src/rebar returns zero matches — no existing call-sites.
The helper's entire logic is `source not in {'backfill_classified', 'classified'}` (or equivalent), a one-liner that requires no abstraction boundary.
findings.finding: `is_authoritative(source)` is a single Boolean predicate that wraps a one-line set-membership check; extracting it as a named, exported module-level function for a single call-site (the authoritative rollup helper tested in `test_registry.py`) is premature abstraction.
scenarios: A maintainer reading the rollup code must jump to the registry module to understand what 'authoritative' means, rather than seeing the set inline.
If the authoritative-source vocabulary changes, the function and its callers must be updated anyway — the abstraction buys nothing over an inline check.
findings.suggested_fix: Inline `source not in {"backfill_classified", "classified"}` at the one call-site; promote to a named function when a second caller appears.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the CLI command (file path) that consumes `REGISTRY` and iterates it for rendering — either add it to the Files section if it is part of this ticket's scope, or explicitly call it out as a future/sibling-ticket consumer so the unenumerated caller is acknowledged.
evidence: What: 'the single source of truth for every metric the command surfaces'
Approach: 'rendering iterates the registry'
Approach: 'the command is written once and never modified as new signals accrue'
Files section lists only src/rebar/metrics/__init__.py, src/rebar/metrics/registry.py, and tests/unit/metrics/test_registry.py — no CLI command file
src/rebar/_commands/ has no metrics command file (confirmed via directory listing)
No file anywhere imports from rebar.metrics (confirmed via grep)
findings.finding: The plan's What and Approach sections describe 'the command' that iterates the registry and 'surfaces' metrics, implying a CLI consumer exists or will be created alongside this ticket. No such command file is listed in Files and no existing file in src/rebar/_commands/ is enumerated as a caller that needs updating — leaving the primary consumer of this registry unenumerated.
findings.location: What section / Approach section / Files section
scenarios: If 'the command' is a sibling ticket's deliverable, the plan should name the dependency so reviewers know this registry is incomplete without it.
If 'the command' is expected to be created in this same ticket, it is missing from the Files list and its consumer relationship to the registry is unenumerated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Consumers' note to the Files or Approach section: e.g., 'The CLI command at src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py (delivered in ticket XYZ) is the primary caller; no existing file requires updating in this ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why a bespoke MetricSpec/REGISTRY is preferred over (or layered on top of) the already-installed `opentelemetry.metrics` SDK, which natively supports declarative metric definitions and dimensional labels equivalent to source/confidence — or confirm the bespoke registry is intentionally a thin internal abstraction that will delegate to OTel at a future step.
evidence: Plan introduces MetricSpec (id, lens, source, confidence, compute), REGISTRY, MetricValue/Unavailable, and is_authoritative with no mention of OTel or any existing metric-registry tool.
opentelemetry.metrics resolves as EXISTS in the installed environment (origin: site-packages/opentelemetry/metrics/__init__.py), including opentelemetry.metrics.Meter.
The source+confidence label contract maps directly to OTel attribute dimensions; is_authoritative segregation maps to OTel Views or exemplar filtering.
ABSENCE: no 'prior art considered' / 'alternatives evaluated' section in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke declarative metric-registry (MetricSpec, REGISTRY, MetricValue/Unavailable, is_authoritative) for engineering signals without considering or ruling out opentelemetry.metrics — which is already installed in the project environment and natively supports declarative metric definitions, dimensional labels (source/confidence as OTel attributes), and instrument-level metadata. This is a novel architecture chosen with no consideration of available prior art.
scenarios: If a future ticket wants to export these metrics to a monitoring backend (Prometheus, Datadog, OTel Collector), the bespoke registry will need a translation layer that OTel would have provided for free.
If OTel attributes are the right model for source/confidence, the is_authoritative helper reimplements filtering that OTel Views already provide.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an 'Alternatives considered' note to the Approach section: e.g. 'OTel instruments were evaluated but not used here because [reason — e.g. this registry is purely for CLI rendering and carries no export intent; OTel will be layered at the export boundary in a follow-on ticket].' If OTel IS the right foundation, replace MetricValue with OTel instrument observations and use OTel attributes for source/confidence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `MetricValue` field specification to the plan (at minimum: `value`, `source: Source`, `confidence: Confidence`, and optionally `unit`/`timestamp`) alongside the `Unavailable` field spec.
evidence: Files: '`MetricValue`/`Unavailable` result types' — both are named but only `Unavailable`'s fields (reason, accruing_since) are described
Approach: 'source+confidence labels ride on every value' — implies MetricValue carries these fields, but the schema is never spelled out
AC-3 tests aggregation by filtering on source, implying MetricValue.source is a field — but this is inferential, not specified
findings.finding: The `MetricValue` success-case schema is never defined: the plan names `MetricValue` as a result type but does not specify what fields it carries (e.g., value, unit, source, confidence, timestamp), leaving its shape completely open.
scenarios: An implementor creates MetricValue as a bare scalar wrapper with no source/confidence fields; the aggregation helper cannot filter on source
The rendering loop (mentioned in Approach) iterates the registry but has no stable field contract to render from

findings.finding: `MetricValue` is named as a result type in the Files/registry.py description alongside `Unavailable`, but its fields are never defined in the plan. Unlike `Unavailable` (whose fields `reason` and `accruing_since` are named in the Approach and AC-2), `MetricValue` has no specified shape.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
